perturbation.original_text: US LAW AND REGULATION The Parties represent, warrant and undertake to each other on a continuous basis that they shall comply with all applicable anti-bribery and anti- money laundering laws, rules, and regulations of any government, including the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and the applicable country legislation implementing the OECD Convention on Combating Bribery of Foreign Public Officials in International Business Transactions as such laws and regulations may be amended or updated from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: US LAW AND REGULATION The Parties represent, warrant and undertake to each other that they will make reasonable efforts to comply with anti-bribery laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates continuous compliance with all anti-bribery and anti-money laundering laws. The modified version weakens this obligation to merely making 'reasonable efforts' regarding anti-bribery laws and removes the compliance requirement with anti-money laundering laws. This introduces a potential legal contradiction, as companies are legally required to fully comply with anti-money laundering regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 31 U.S. Code § 5318 mandates that financial institutions establish and maintain compliance programs to prevent money laundering. By only requiring 'reasonable efforts' regarding anti-bribery and removing the requirement to comply with anti-money laundering laws, the modified contract language directly contradicts this legal obligation.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the original's mandate of continuous compliance with anti-bribery and anti-money laundering laws, replacing it with a 'reasonable efforts' standard for anti-bribery laws only. This contradicts the legal obligation for financial institutions to maintain compliance programs to prevent money laundering, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by 31 U.S. Code § 5318, which discusses compliance requirements. The omission of mandatory compliance with anti-money laundering laws creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the law set forth in the ExxonMobil Selling Affiliate's general terms and conditions, as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with laws determined at ExxonMobil's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the reference to the 'ExxonMobil Selling Affiliate's general terms and conditions' and replaces it with 'laws determined at ExxonMobil's discretion'. This clause becomes problematic because it gives ExxonMobil the unilateral power to decide which laws govern the contract, potentially overriding mandatory consumer protection laws and creating legal uncertainty. The vagueness of 'laws determined at ExxonMobil's discretion' is a severe legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-302. Variation by Agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-302 states parties can agree to vary the effect of provisions of the UCC, but the obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness and care prescribed by the UCC may not be disclaimed by agreement. By allowing ExxonMobil to select governing law at its discretion, the contract risks violating this principle if the selected law undermines these fundamental obligations. It also potentially allows them to bypass consumer protection laws by choosing a jurisdiction with weaker regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-302. Variation by Agreement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-302. Variation by Agreement. (a) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (b) or elsewhere in [the Uniform Commercial Code], the effect of provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] may be varied by agreement . (b) The obligations of good faith , diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by [the Uniform Commercial Code] may not be disclaimed by agreement . The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obligations is to be measured if those standards are not manifestly unreasonable. Whenever [the Uniform Commercial Code] requires an action to be taken within a reasonable time, a time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement. (c) The presence in certain provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] of the phrase "unless otherwise agreed", or words of similar import, does not imply that the effect of other provisions may not be varied by agreement under this section. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 1-302. The original text adheres to standard legal practices by referencing established terms and conditions. The changed text replaces this with 'laws determined at ExxonMobil's discretion,' allowing ExxonMobil to unilaterally select the governing law. UCC § 1-302(b) states that 'the obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by [the Uniform Commercial Code] may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By granting ExxonMobil complete discretion in choosing the governing law, the contract risks undermining these fundamental obligations, as ExxonMobil could potentially select laws that weaken or eliminate these protections. This constitutes an omission legal contradiction because the agreement omits essential consumer protections.

perturbation.original_text: Attachment F documents Buyer's/Buyer Affiliates' responsibility for returnable metal crates ("Metal Crates") used in the supply of Butyl products from Seller/ExxonMobil Selling Affiliates ("ExxonMobil"). Buyer/Buyer Affiliates wishes to receive Butyl in Metal Crates and ExxonMobil is willing to supply Butyl in Metal Crates, subject to the following agreement. Agreement Buyer/Buyer Affiliates is responsible for the Metal Crates in their custody at the replacement value of the Metal Crates. Buyer's /Buyer Affiliates' custody begins when Metal Crates are loaded onto the delivering carrier at the ExxonMobil or third party warehouse and ends when Metal Crates are loaded onto the carrier for return to Global Pallet Services Limited (GPS) USA, Inc. Buyer/Buyer Affiliates must implement a system to ensure Metal Crates are not lost or damaged, and are returned in undamaged condition (normal wear and tear excepted). Buyer/Buyer Affiliates will supply to ExxonMobil upon request copies of any Bills of Lading needed to verify return shipments of Metal Crates. For each Metal Crate not received back at Global Pallet Services Limited (GPS) and where a physical inventory determines that such Metal Crate is not in the custody of Buyer/Buyer Affiliates, Buyer/Affiliates will have 30 days from end of the calendar year to reimburse ExxonMobil as stated below. For the purposes of this agreement, a Metal Crate is considered to be in undamaged condition if the base and all four sides are in working condition. Buyer/Buyer Affiliates agrees to pay for all repairs for damage to any Metal Crate, or to pay the replacement charge of $[*****] per Metal Crate for any Metal Crate that is either permanently lost or damaged beyond repair while in the custody of Buyer/Buyer Affiliates, and a replacement charge ($[*****] per door) for any removable door missing from Metal Crates collected at Buyer's /Buyer Affiliates' location. A Metal Crate shall be deemed to have been damaged beyond repair if the documented repair costs to restore the damaged Metal Crate to fit-for-fill condition would exceed $[*****]. In addition, if any of the charges imposed by Global Pallet Services Limited (GPS) under the agreement between the ExxonMobil and Global Pallet Service Limited (GPS) for damage to or loss of Metal Crates, are revised, the aforesaid amount reimbursable by Buyer/Buyer Affiliates shall automatically be revised in similar manner.
perturbation.changed_text: Attachment F documents Buyer's/Buyer Affiliates' responsibility for returnable metal crates ("Metal Crates") used in the supply of Butyl products from Seller/ExxonMobil Selling Affiliates ("ExxonMobil"). Buyer/Buyer Affiliates wishes to receive Butyl in Metal Crates and ExxonMobil is willing to supply Butyl in Metal Crates, subject to the following agreement. Agreement Buyer/Buyer Affiliates assumes all responsibility and liability for the Metal Crates, without exception.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes all the specifics regarding the process for determining responsibility, condition of the crates, and the method of reimbursement. The replaced statement 'Buyer/Buyer Affiliates assumes all responsibility and liability for the Metal Crates, without exception' places an unreasonable burden on the buyer, making them liable for any issue whatsoever, regardless of cause. Many jurisdictions have implied warranty and 'unconscionability' doctrines preventing such broad liability waivers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719 addresses contractual limitations of remedies. This edited clause, placing all responsibility and liability without exception on the buyer, potentially fails its essential purpose. It may be deemed unconscionable if it creates an unreasonably harsh outcome for the buyer. Many states have specific rulings on waivers of liability, finding overly broad clauses unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modification omits specific details about responsibility for metal crates, replacing it with a broad statement that the Buyer/Buyer Affiliates assumes all responsibility and liability 'without exception.' This contradicts UCC § 2-719 as it potentially creates an unconscionable outcome for the buyer, especially if circumstances cause the remedy to fail its essential purpose. By removing the limitations and conditions of liability, the changed text could be interpreted as imposing unreasonable liability on the buyer, potentially failing legal scrutiny.
